data.title: Regenxbio's RGX-121 Could Become The New Standard Of Care In Hunter Syndrome
data.description:
Regenxbio (NASDAQ:RGNX) is a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company focusing on gene therapies. Founded in 2008, the core technology of the company is the delivery of rescue genes using AAV payload. A growing number of approved drugs are based on this well established AAV approach. In May 2025, Regenxbio announced that the FDA will settle on the approval (PDUFA) of RGX-121 in the treatment of Hunter syndrom (HS) on November 9th 2025. In this article, we propose to review the Regenxbio submission and to explain why, at ELAM1, we think RGNX could be a Buy in advance of the FDA decision in November.

Hunter Syndrome and RGX-121

Mucopolysaccharidosis type II, also called Hunter Syndrome (HS), is a genetic disease progressively affecting all systems, leading to multi-organ dysfunction. The disease is due to a deficiency in iduronate-2-sulfatase, leading to an accumulation of glycosaminoglycans (GAG). The only existing treatment is Elaprase, a weekly intravenous infusion, to replace the deficient enzyme. Its use suggest both short-term and long-term benefits in patients. However, the impact on the neurological symptoms remains limited, thus leaving room for improvement.

In line with their technology and the current treatment landscape, RGNX developed RGX-121. It is an AAV-based drug designed to deliver the lacking enzyme in the central nervous system. RGNX is now seeking approval for this drug. From their corporate presentation, CAMPSIITE data will be used to support the announced submission to the FDA. Let's review the data to see if we can expect an approval for the drug. CAMPSIITE is designed in two parts: Phase I/II/III and one pivotal phase.

Phase I/II/III consists of a dose-finding design investigating the accumulation of GAG in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), a surrogate for neurological progression, safety, neurodevelopment evolution, and the need for enzyme replacement therapy (ERT). 15 patients (4 months to 5 years old) with severe neurologic impairments were enrolled to receive one unique dose of RGX-121 directly into the central nervous system. They were either under ERT or naïve to the treatment. Looking at the safety profile, no severe adverse events were related either to RGX-121 or to the administration procedure at all dose levels, suggesting a strong safety profile for the drug. Also, no side effects led to discontinuation or severe neuropathy.

Looking at its efficacy, a reduction of more than 80% in GAG in the CSF was observed up to two years after infusion of the highest dose. This suggests a potential effect of the drug over the neurological progression of the disease. However, it is the results from the clinical evaluation that, to us, matter the most. Indeed, only patients with a baseline above 2 standard deviations benefited from the treatment. This is of importance because it could preclude the clinical adoption for patients with more severe impairments. Finally, looking at the ERT data, 4 patients out of 5 remained ERT free at the highest dose. However, caution should be made since ERT free is either a discontinuation of the ERT or remaining ERT naïve. Down the road, all these data suggest a clinical efficacy for patients with light to moderate neurologic disease.

Looking at the pivotal phase results, 10 patients from 4 months to 56 months benefited from the one-time treatment. Only one severe adverse event consisting of elevated liver enzymes was reported and easily managed by steroids, supporting its safety profile. Regarding the efficacy, results were in line with the dose-finding study. Indeed, investigators observed a reduction of 86% in GAG in the CSF. This reduction was sustained for up to two years, suggesting no need for repeated infusion of RGX-121. Looking at the neurodevelopmental function, the press release only states "the majority of patients were shown to be exceeding expectations in neurodevelopmental function compared to natural history data up to four years," consistent with the previous data. However, no additional details are shown.

Given the strong surrogate results and a good safety profile for this disease with close to no treatment options, an approval in November by the FDA could be expected. Let's now explore the financial consequences of such approval.

Financials

From their Q1 2025 report, the company holds $272M in cash. Given a cash burn rate of around $70M per quarter, this cash will allow it to finance activities until the second half of 2026. The company also holds total assets of $490M against total liabilities of $216M. With a market capitalization of around $420M, it brings its price-to-book ratio to around 1.85, suggesting a good point to buy and mitigate the risk of downside linked to the clinical stage of the company.

Regarding the potential approval of RGX-121, a positive decision would enhance the stock price by two means. First, through their collaboration with NS Pharma, RGNX is expected to receive $40M on the approval and up to $660M on sales milestones while retaining double-digit royalties on the sales of RGX-121. This alone would be able to trigger a rally in the stock price. Now, looking at the expected sales, 500 patients present HS in the United States. Prices for one-time gene therapies are high and could reach up to a few millions. Given its long-term, sustained, efficacy, a price of $2M is not out of reach for RGX-121. Therefore, sales of up to $1B are reachable. However, penetration will not be completed, and RGNX is only entitled to a double-digit royalties. Therefore, expected revenue for RGNX could be as low as $100M (50% penetration, 10% royalties). Thus, we think the potential upside for the stock price will mainly result from the anticipated milestones and will be around 10% to 30% in case of approval.

Risks to the thesis

A major risk to the thesis is of course a non-approval by the FDA. Indeed, to this date, the FDA has never approved an HS treatment based on GAG reduction data. Therefore, despite good results and a lack of treatment in HS, approval remains a challenge for the company. In addition, the company has multiple programs in hot fields such as Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy. Results in trials in these fields could also results in significant stock price variation, independently of RGX-121's fate.

Conclusion

RGX-121 is a promising drug in the HS field by aiming for a neurological improvement in patients. While the approval remains uncertain, it could trigger milestones for the company leading to a stock price appreciation.

data.title: Gold Fails To Break Resistance In Risk-On Market Environment
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Gold had been climbing steadily despite the overall risk-on sentiment this week. However, this morning’s stronger-than-expected Non-Farm Payrolls report failed to give the safe-haven asset any additional lift.

The rejection at higher levels suggests a lower likelihood of a retest of the all-time highs, which remain at $3,500 - although with this year's volatility, everything is possible.

The Bullion, which was up 3% at its weekly highs is now up only 1.26% - Let's dive into a technical analysis as we stand on the current pivot.

Gold Technical Analysis from Daily to Hourly charts

Gold Daily Chart

Gold broke out of its descending daily channel on Monday, supported by early-week US Dollar weakness. However, the streak of consecutive bearish daily candles is undermining momentum toward its all-time highs.

The Daily RSI is neutral though above its 50 level, which still gives it a somewhat bullish bias.

The MA 50 on the daily is still catching up to the rise that occured through the latter part of May.

Standing right at the current pivot of $3,330, we still have to observe if a prolongation of the rejection leads to a re-entering of the Daily Channel.

Gold 4H Chart

Gold prices are looking to retest the higher band of the Daily Channel, located at 3,310 as it rejects the 4H MA 20.

One hurdle to look at that may slow a potential down-move within the Channel would be the MA 200 located at the confluence with the 3,300 psychological zone.

Prices which are contained within these 2 key MAs infer some indecision, however we may expect some more clarity throughout the weekly close.

A bounce on the upper channel line will hint to another test of the 3,375 to 3,390 Resistance zone, while a break below the 200 MA points toward the 3,275 to 3,290 Support Zone

Watch the market sentiment, as a further move towards all-time highs in US stock indices may just add to more pressure for the Bullion.

Gold 1H Chart

Gold is entering an oversold level as we approach the immediate support zone located between 3,305 and 3,315.

The descent is still fairly strong, therefore watch for any particular switch in market sentiment:

A positive tone may give more strength to sellers, while a risk-off move towards the week-end will provide support; things are still unclear for now but the current mood is positive.

We can observe some harmonic patterns towards the correction in the precious metal as the move up happened after an inverted Head and Shoulders, touching the $3,400 psychological zone before reverting on the Trump-Xi conversation headlines.We are now observing another potential measured move on the way down (purple squares).

Also watch for potential consolidation around the 3,330 pivot.Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: May Jobs Boost Confidence, Squash Hopes Of A Rate Cut
data.description:
NEW YORK (June 6) The Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) announced 139,000 new jobs for April, according to the Establishment Survey, a collection of job creation data from businesses replying to the survey. The number was somewhat above the consensus estimate of 126,000.

March and April jobs creation figures were also revised downward by 95,000 net jobs. The average new non-farms jobs creation figure for the last three months is 135,000 new jobs.

The BLS’s Household Survey, which polls the number of people taking jobs, and is viewed as eliminating workers holding more than one job, printed nearly the same as the Establishment Survey at 188,000 new jobs.

The unemployment rate and the U6 rate both held steady at 4.2% and 7.8%, respectively. The “U6” is the percentage of the population that is unemployed, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force, plus total employed part time for economic reasons, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force. Some 625,000 people left the labor force, pushing the Labor Force Participation Rate down slightly, to 62.4% from 62.6%, in April.

Let’s look at our exclusive schedule of April and May Jobs Creation by Average Weekly Wages:

Jobs creation was led, once again, by what we call, “government-adjacent” jobs: Private Education & Health Services and Healthcare and Social Services, which tend to be funded by governmental programs, followed by lower wage jobs in hospitality. The low-wage Hospitality sector, where average wages are just $575/week, produced 48,000 new jobs.

Retail lost 6,500 jobs, as did manufacturing, which lost 8,000 jobs. We do not include “official” government jobs payrolls in our chart. There were only 1,000 such jobs lost in May, despite the work of DOGE.

Economy Generally

Based on today’s numbers, we believe the financial media’s chorus call about a tariff-induced, self-inflicted, recession continue to be overstated.

First quarter GDP printed basically flat, at -0.2% in the second estimate. But so called “Real GDP” - GDP minus elements of “noise,” and formally referred to as “Real final sales to private domestic purchasers" - printed at 2.5% in the revised estimate, so relatively stable since around 2022.

We have thought for some time that the threat to the economy from the tariffs is considerably overblown and largely driven by media hand-wringing. We continue to believe that seller margins, not revenue, will be affected by President Trump’s tariffs, as we explained in this article last November. Business income will be affected, at least for a year or two.

For China, we think the increasing collapse of China’s economy we discussed here will cause China’s prices for manufactured goods to fall so that the cost of Chinese goods, after tariffs, will be within a couple of standard deviations of the 2024 average prices to American consumers. Walmart (WMT) and, we expect a number of other large US importers, will use the buyers market to force their wholesale price from Chinese manufacturers down considerably.

Meanwhile, companies hoping to maintain market share will maintain prices and bear much of the cost of the remaining burden of tariffs. The reduced margins will obviously affect the stock price of such companies for as long as the tariffs are in place, but we think that inflation expectations will remain anchored about where they are now. Indeed, the 5 year expectation remains around 2.25, about where it was in December, 2022.

We explained back in January that a recession was almost assured after the 3-month/10-year inverted yield curve resolved. We’re not yet ready to dismiss that thinking at the middle of the second quarter, but we are reducing our level of confidence. The upending of the global “free trade” paradigm for the “fair trade” paradigm envisioned by President Trump will assuredly be a rocky road, but we’re doubting the histrionics of the cable news business networks that almost uniformly claim a recession is imminent.

That said, we do see some weakness in the JOLTS data so that jobs are not as easy to come by and, as we see in the chart above, manufacturers have borne some job losses, as have retailers.

None of these data points are terribly disconcerting, but they both cause us to be cautious about dismissing our earlier recession prediction. We'll reconsider it in the third quarter GDP report.

We think the tariff situation will be resolved by the end of the third quarter for most of our major trading partners, but not for China. That said, we are concerned that the European leadership establishment, which mostly despises the conservative populist/nationalist movement that Trump embodies, may resist reforms, particularly with the non-tariff barriers that are elemental in their mercantilist trade policy.

We also fear the EU’s governing elite will try to play a “China Card” to limit the U.S. negotiating strength. China has lifted sanctions against members of the European Parliament that had been imposed after the EU accused China of human rights violations of the Muslim Uyghurs in Xinjiang. European leaders are happy with the status quo ante and would very much like to revert to it and continue their mercantilist trade policies.

With China as a backstop, Europe may feel happy to resist the Trump tariffs, collectively holding firm through the 2026 midterm elections and hoping U.S. voters will flip the House or Senate or both to the Democrats and thus hobble the “America First” Trump trade agenda with an impeachment. That said, we doubt the EU will be willing to exacerbate its existing trade balance with China by buying more Chinese goods. As we wrote earlier this week, that leaves China in an economically untenable position unless it comes to an agreement with the USA on tariffs and other trade matters.

Prognostications

Over the long term, the aggressive Trump Administration tariff policy could be effective in reshoring and near-shoring jobs that went overseas starting with the passage of the WTO and NAFTA. Apple, Honda, and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company are but three of the companies that have now pledged to manufacture in the USA.

But in the short term, tariffs will adversely affect consumers, particularly those lower income workers. Christmas this year will likely be more gift cards and experiences than packages under the tree as there may be shortages in goods. But a Christmas 2026 would look far better if promises for “no tax on tips,” “no tax on overtime,” and “no tax on Social Security” are fulfilled in “great, big, beautiful bill” Trump and congressional Republicans have promised to deliver.

We believe the House budget of that bill is on the bubble in the Senate, but we’re confident the conference agreement will get it into law. The economy is in far better shape than we anticipated it would be. We assess 2025 Q2 will print in the range of 0.75% growth, +/- 25 bps, down a quarter point from our April jobs report.

Other Data Points

These are data points we use to assess the overall standing of the economy and are provided here for information only.

GDP printed at a negative 0.2% annual growth rate for 2025Q1, in the second estimate released May 29th. Core GDP, or final sales to domestic purchasers, which eliminates some of the “noise” in the GDP report, like inventories and net exports, printed at 2.5%, down 50 bps from the advance estimate. Core PCE Inflation, ex-food and fuel, was released May 29tj and printed at a 2.5% annual rate in April.

The Institute for Supply Management's Manufacturer's Purchasing Managers Index (PMI) printed at 48.5 in May, down from 48.7 in April. Prices are increasing slower while Manufacturing is contracting faster. The March ISM Services Index, printed at 49.9 in May, down from 51.6 in April.

The Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey ("JOLTS") for April printed Tuesday, with 391,000 fewer job openings than February.

Privately-owned housing units authorized by building permits in April were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1,412,000. This is 4.7 percent below the revised March rate of 1,481,000 and is 3.2 percent below the April 2024 rate of 1,459,000.

Privately-owned housing starts in April were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1,361,000. This is 1.6 percent (±11.8 percent)* above the revised March estimate of 1,339,000, but is 1.7 percent (±9.2 percent)* below the April 2024 rate of 1,385,000.

Personal income increased $210.1 billion (0.8 percent at a monthly rate) in April, according to estimates released today by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. Disposable personal income (DPI)—personal income less personal current taxes—increased $189.4 billion (0.8 percent).

The PCE price index for April increased 2.1 percent. Excluding food and energy, the PCE price index increased 2.5 percent from one year ago.

The May RCM/TIPP Economic Optimism Index , reported April 1, printed at 49.1, down from 49.8% in March. The May Index has not yet printed.

____________________________________

Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political/geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe to be under-performing. While we have never written about any companies that are our clients and do not include companies we write about here in our personal investment portfolios, we do provide here strategies that we would have recommended were the companies our clients. Others discuss new management strategies of non-client companies we believe will fail. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions regarding such companies here, however, assume the company will not change).

data.title: Rivian Automotive A Victim Of Poor Timing As Battery Electrics Struggle To Find Buyers
data.description:
The 2018 Los Angeles Auto Show was memorable for the public debut of Rivian Automotive, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:RIVN) R1T pickup and R1S SUV. I recall standing with scores of fellow analysts and journalists when the wraps were removed from the two prototypes. A pleasant buzz rippled through the crowd. The designs struck me as unusually handsome. And I guess others felt the same, at least so it seemed by heads nodding, scattered applause and expressions of admiration. A star was born!

Fast-forward nearly seven years. Rivian is building vehicles. But the company is struggling, as are many BEV startups; and, crucially, consumers and U.S. regulators have weighed in: At least for the moment, both groups are measurably less enthusiastic about the role BEVs are destined to play in personal transportation than those analysts were at the time.

Amid the initial consumer excitement and regulatory fervor to incentivize BEVs, RIVN went public in 2021 with a sale of 153 million shares that raised nearly $12 billion. Since then, the company has burned through more than $19 billion in capital. Within a few months share prices crashed from the vicinity of $175 to the neighborhood of $25. Today they’re scraping along at just under $14, having bottomed at under $9.

Euro Backstop

In order to forestall a liquidity emergency, Rivian entered a $5.8 billion strategic alliance with Volkswagen AG (OTCPK:VWAGY), the German giant, trading Rivian’s software and electronic architecture tech for VW’s financial depth.

Nevertheless, RIVN believers seem to abound, many believing that once the company’s second assembly plant in Georgia opens and begins building smaller and less pricey versions of the original, the company will become consistently cash positive and then profitable. In the fourth quarter of 2024 the company managed to post its first gross profit. This was followed by a second straight gross profit for the first quarter of 2025, a milestone that enabled the automaker to qualify for $1 billion in funding from VW this month.

The forecast for 2025 vehicle output has been lowered to 40,000 to 46,000 from an earlier forecast of 46,000 to 51,000 due to various factors, including rising competition from new BEV models and the necessity of shutting down its main Normal, Ill., assembly plant for a month to prepare for the expected manufacture of the smaller and less pricey R2 model early next year and an R3 later on.

The Trump administration has taken a dim view of federal incentives for battery-powered vehicles, plus a policy of stiff tariffs on imported parts and materials. A $6.6 billion federal loan, passed in the closing days of the Biden administration for construction of a second Rivian assembly plant 40 miles east of Atlanta, Ga., could be on the chopping block, public officials have acknowledged.

Consumer Skeptics

Nor has consumer response to BEVs been particularly encouraging. The latest survey conducted by AAA and released last week exposed U.S. car “buyers continued hesitation” to purchase BEVs:

“Only 16% of U.S. adults report being very likely or likely to purchase a fully electric vehicle (EV) as their next car, the lowest percentage recorded of EV interest since 2019. The percentage of consumers indicating they would be unlikely or very unlikely to purchase an EV rose from 51% to 63%, the highest since 2022.”

Consumers have consistently highlighted concerns about high prices, battery range and insufficient charging infrastructure. Worries about the high price of gasoline, meanwhile, have moderated as the national average price of a gallon of unleaded has dropped to $3.14 a gallon from $3.48 a gallon a year ago. In 2022, unleaded prices reached $5 a gallon, prompting many to consider BEVs for fuel savings.

Beyond tariffs, the outlook for which is gloomy, another potential barrier to BEV sales materialized in early April when “China imposed new restrictions on exports of rare earth minerals, its latest salvo in the intensifying trade standoff with the US.” Rare earth minerals such as samarium, gadolinium, terbium, dysprosium, lutetium, scandium and yttrium are essential in the production of semiconductors, military equipment and electric motors and other components used by BEVs.

"Without reliable access to these elements and magnets, automotive suppliers will be unable to produce critical automotive components, including automatic transmissions, throttle bodies, alternators, various motors, sensors, seat belts, speakers, lights, motors, power steering, and cameras," MEMA (Motor Equipment Manufacturers Association) and the Alliance for Automotive Innovation wrote to the Trump administration, which was first reported by Reuters.

Pretty Faces

Initial investor interest in RIVN following the IPO in 2021 was intense, partly due to the vehicle’s pleasing visual design, partly due to rising consumer and regulatory interest in BEVs and partly because R.J. Scaringe, the company’s young and photogenic founder, was admired as an innovator – in some ways like you-know-who. For all the world, RIVN appeared to be an enterprise that might sweep along with the momentum of Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), with a market capitalization to match.

Then reality set in. In October of last year, for example, a parts shortage traced to an inability to source copper was blamed for lowering production output by about 10,000 vehicles. As Elon Musk discovered when he began sleeping on Tesla’s factory floor in order to personally address difficulties at the assembly plant, manufacturing vehicles is extremely tricky and can be brought to a halt by a single part shortage.

What’s breathtakingly obvious to Wall Street analysts (who average a Hold rating), Seeking Alpha analysts (who average a Hold rating) and to Seeking Alpha’s Quant system (also with a Hold), this stock should be avoided – at least for the moment. I rate it a Sell.

What keeps RIVN from quickly becoming a $2 or $3 stock from its current level between $13 and $14 is the relationship with VW. Volkswagen relies on the partnership due to Rivian’s critical technical expertise. But unless Rivian can resolve some of the risk factors that becloud its future, it could find itself eventually absorbed into the German automaker, like many other brands. German-flavored Rivians? Maybe. Whether such an outcome would work well for equity holders is unpredictable.

data.title: Can Canada Emerge As An Energy Superpower?
data.description:
The Canadian government has big ambitions to make the country a global energy superpower. It’s one of the key pledges being pitched by Prime Minister Mark Carney. Hussein Allidina, Managing Director and Head of Commodities at TD Asset Management, discusses the challenges and opportunities that may come in making that goal a reality.

Transcript

Kim Parlee - The Canadian government has big ambitions to make this country a global energy superpower. It was one of the key themes highlighted in a meeting this week between Prime Minister Mark Carney and the country's premiers. But what's it actually going to take to get us to that point? And does this initiative create potential investment opportunities?

Joining us to break it all down is Hussein Allidina. He is Managing Director and Head of Commodities at TD Asset Management. How are you?

Hussein Allidina - I'm great. Excited.

Two parts I want to talk about, I think, more is Canada and the global energy superpower, and then just the idea of how much energy is going to be needed as we move ahead. So these are my two questions over the next little while.

At a high level, what does it mean for Canada to become, assuming it's not, a global energy superpower? What does it take? What's in the way?

So we're the fourth largest crude producer globally. So I'd say we are a superpower.

Kim Parlee - Agreed.

Hussein Allidina - But I think that we've likely been punching below our weight, right? We've been blessed, not just in energy, but from a commodity perspective, we've been endowed with a tremendous amount of resource. And I think we've neglected the development of that resource for different reasons over the course of the last decade.

I think Prime Minister Carney's refreshing take on it is welcome. I think we're going to need the energy. We're going to need the commodities. So I think there will be a market for it.

But it does take time. And we've talked about this before-- the reason a commodity cycle exists is because it takes time for supply to respond. So even with the best of intentions, which I think the prime minister has and the energy minister now has, it takes time to build out.

Kim Parlee - Can we just elaborate on that a little bit? Because I think it's interesting for people to know, because we hear-- I know TD Economics came out, this was a pipeline, but they said I think it was 18 years to bring a mine online in Canada.

Hussein Allidina - Yep.

Kim Parlee - But when you and I were chatting earlier, you were saying even if we had all the approvals, everything we needed to get done and just started--

Hussein Allidina - Yeah.

It takes two to three years to build a pipeline.

So let's just take an example of TMX, which came on last year. That project took 11 years from idea to the first sort of commercial use.

Kim Parlee - Yeah.

Hussein Allidina - Northern Gateway took 14 years, 12 years before it was canceled.

Kim Parlee - Right.

Hussein Allidina - Right? So there is a process that we have to move through. And I imagine that the federal and provincial government, if there is appetite, can expedite that. But there are physical challenges, physical constraints associated with laying pipe or building upstream, downstream production. And that stuff takes time.

Two to three years is probably very optimistic. 11 years might be on the long end of things. But can we get pipelines and infrastructure built in five to seven years? I think so if there is appetite to do that.

Kim Parlee - Yeah. As you, as someone who watches commodity prices, how does that affect how you look at things? It's material, I assume, at some level later on. But how are you thinking about it?

Hussein Allidina - So I'm excited about it as a Canadian, because I think it's phenomenal for Canada's potential GDP, what it means for the dollar, what it means for GDP per capita. It doesn't make me any less bearish commodities near-term because it takes so long to bring this production to market if things go well. Generally speaking, I'd say-- I'm sure we'll talk about the oil market later-- but the focus of market participants, broadly speaking, is the next 6 to 12 months, maybe 18 months.

Kim Parlee - Yep.

Hussein Allidina - Are there folks trading, oil five years forward? Yes, but not meaningfully. And, again, I think even five years forward is probably the earliest where we see the supply-demand impact of policy changes which are happening today.

Kim Parlee - Yeah. And so when you are building pipelines and making the plans in terms of what the paybacks are and revenues, you have to look at prices five years out, not six months out, to your point-- and you see transitions happening with LNG, nuclear. I think Meta (META) just signed a deal with--

Hussein Allidina - Constellation (CEG).

Kim Parlee - A 20-year deal. So what do you longer term see for the demand for oil?

Hussein Allidina - So I think there's two things there. So on the long term, the demand for oil is going to be highly, highly correlated and a function of GDP growth, first and foremost, and then your alternatives. Today, whether we like it or not, oil is still one of the cheapest forms of energy.

It's the most available. It's secure. It's easy to move. And I don't see demand, we've talked about this for years, I don't see demand for oil peaking the way that some have argued will happen before 2030. I think oil demand continues to grow if the economy continues to move.

On the price level that is discounted when you're thinking about the DCF of a company or the economics of a pipeline, oftentimes, the forward curve will be used. So we'll look at the forward curve and use that as the expected price.

Kim Parlee - Yeah.

Hussein Allidina - But the forward curve is not a predictor of future price. So we use it, or analysts will use it, but, ultimately, if you take the forward curve or if you look at the forward curve, you have this sort of misguided idea that oil prices are decreasing in the future. The weakness in forward prices will discourage investment, all else equal, which makes it all the harder.

Kim Parlee - And what about, if I just say power-- oil is one way of powering the economy, but the demand for power overall for the next little while is you to see up and to the right-- or is it?

Hussein Allidina - Yeah. No, I think it is. So, globally, demand for power will grow with GDP. So as an economy industrializes, their power demand increases.

Kim Parlee - But AI and--

Hussein Allidina - But if you look in the developed markets-- so in the US, for example, power demand for the better part of 20 years was flat to down because we were exporting our manufacturing, our power demand elsewhere. Today, if we look at power demand in the US over the course of the last 12, 16 months, for the first time, it's been increasing.

Kim Parlee - It's been increasing because, as you said, ChatGPT or AI-related demand, as well as energy transition on the margin-- you're seeing substitution out of ICE vehicles into EV vehicles. I think the outlook for power is quite constructive globally and locally because of the growth in the economy together with what seems like insatiable demand from AI.

Bring it back to Canada. LNG, of course, is something that we're focused on here as well, too. And I think the first exports from Kitimat are getting closer. But Canada, are we not a later player on the LNG stage?

Hussein Allidina - Yeah. So globally, your largest LNG suppliers are the US and Qatar. And it does take time to build liquefaction capacity. And we're definitely behind the ball. But, again, if we look at the world and we anticipate that GDP is going to continue to increase, power demand is going to continue to increase, a lot of that power today is met with coal.

Part of the reason that power is met with coal is because China, India, et cetera have coal, and security of supply has become an issue in the last couple of years. I think that Canada should develop and continue to develop their LNG. We should try to do it quicker than we have been doing it.

Kim Parlee - Yeah.

Hussein Allidina - But, ultimately, we're going to need to grow power supply. Nuclear will fill part of that gap. But you're talking about a 10-year window where you're not going to have meaningful nuclear additions. Natural gas can fill that void. And even post when we have that nuclear running, potentially, you're still going to need gas.

Kim Parlee - Yeah.

Hussein Allidina - If you're a China or an India, today, you're importing from Qatar-- it's a relatively long route-- or you're importing from the US, it's not clear that the US is always going to be there to export to you. So Canada can play a role in diversifying the supply chain for Asia. And, geographically, we happen to be well situated.

Kim Parlee - I want to bring it back more into current times. And we have some horrible fires that are taking place, actually, right across the country. But specifically, we're seeing a lot in Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba. And we're seeing a shutdown of a lot of oil production. Is that material to the oil market?

Hussein Allidina - So a horrible situation.

Kim Parlee - Yeah.

Hussein Allidina - Right now, there's about 300 to 400,000 barrels a day of oil shut in. That's largely, I believe, Cenovus, MEG, and the Christina Lake project, I believe. Those are not trivial volumes, right?

So the market, maybe to contrast, the market was quite concerned about the incremental barrels that OPEC was adding to the market.

Kim Parlee - Right.

Hussein Allidina - They added 414,000 barrels a day, just for some perspective--

Kim Parlee - Yeah, yeah.

Hussein Allidina - In May, June, and July. If we lose this 300, 400,000 barrels a day for a period of time, that offsets that. The quality of the oil is also very important. So Canada produces a medium heavy barrel.

A lot of the production growth that we've seen globally of late, which has come out of the US shale, has been a light sweet barrel. You need those medium barrels to mix with the light barrels to put into the refinery, to make the gasoline and diesel that we consume. Before I came down, actually, it looks like there's some rain in the forecast, which is going to provide some reprieve and might lend to some production returning to market.

Hopefully that production doesn't stay offline for a meaningful amount of time. But barrels lost are barrels lost.

Kim Parlee - Yeah. What is your short term look on oil right now, let's just say in the next three months, six months, given OPEC and what's happening elsewhere?

Hussein Allidina - So it's been hard. And we've talked before about this disconnect in supply-demand fundamentals, inventories and price. For the better part, I would say, Kim, for the last 16 months, the market has been anticipating, consensus has been anticipating these meaningful builds in inventories. And that's got the market, despite the robust near-term fundamentals, has got the market relatively bearish.

We haven't seen those builds. If we look at the first half of this year, inventories have built, but not meaningfully more than normal, and definitely not as much as the market expected. On paper, when we look at balances through the end of the year, we're supposed to see sizable builds by the end of the year. In the fourth quarter, we might see two million barrels a day of builds, which would be meaningful. My caveat is that we've been expecting these builds for a very long time, and they haven't materialized.

Kim Parlee - And have the builds not materialized because the supply has been lower, or the demand? Which side?

Hussein Allidina - So a couple of weeks ago, the IEA came out-- not to get too technical-- but in the oil balance, you have your supply, your demand. And that should equal the change in inventory.

Kim Parlee - Right.

Hussein Allidina - There's a line item on the balance that's called miscellaneous to balance, which is the plug.

Kim Parlee - Always bad things come from miscellaneous to balance everywhere.

Hussein Allidina - So they came back out-- two weeks ago, they came out and they said, by the way, demand in 2022, 2023, 2024 was higher than we thought. So they put it into demand. I think we have to be very mindful that we're talking about a market, Kim, that's 105 million barrels a day. And when we talk about the margin, the surplus or the deficit, we're talking about 500,000 barrels a day, a million barrels a day.

So we're applying a lot of precision, but, in reality, it's 105 million barrel per day market. And we don't have complete transparency on inventories globally.

Kim Parlee - Right.

Hussein Allidina - Today, at $65 a barrel, I'm less bullish than I was two weeks ago in the $50s. I think, on paper, there is more upside risk in the near term because of geopolitics and the potential supply disruptions that stem from that. But I'm definitely concerned that growth slows and what that means, ultimately, for balances later this year. I'm not in the $40, $50 camp, but I'm also not in the $80, $90 camp unless we have some sort of supply related disruption.

Kim Parlee - What are you watching-- sorry, when you're watching, when you talk about-- there's a lot of things to pull on there. So from a geopolitical standpoint, what's going to move markets? And then also from a demand standpoint-- because as we all just wait and see, what does trade look, really, when deals happen, don't happen, whatever, everything. We're all tired of it.

Hussein Allidina - So on geopolitics, I would say that two months ago, consensus was very much, hey, Trump is going to forge a deal between Russia-Ukraine, and there's going to be something positive that happens with Iran in the Middle East. Both of those things look far less likely today.

Kim Parlee - Ukraine's special forces has a lot to do with what happened lately.

Hussein Allidina - And Iran-- Iran has been very clear that the red line for them is that we're going to continue to enrich. The red line for the US is that, no, you're not. So I'm not sure how that plays out. And I imagine there is some political gamesmanship taking place.

But the likelihood that Iranian barrels are going to return en masse to the market, as some people are expecting, I think is lower probability than maybe what some folks thought a couple of months ago. So there's a lot of production at risk.

We haven't talked about Libya, which is also having issues right now, in addition to the challenges in Canada with the wildfires. A lot of supply at risk and very tight balances. Now, I don't know how bearish I can get on this idea that, hey, demand is going to fall apart later this year, and I'm going to get phenomenal supply growth.

The weakness in price that we've seen over the course of the last several weeks has already lent to a reduction in the number of rigs drilling for oil in the Permian Basin. If you look at frack crews, the number of folks that are out there working, that number has fallen off meaningfully as well. So I think we have to remember that the market has, I think, a tendency of maybe being more bearish on demand than we should be.

And we definitely are, at least over the course of the last 10 years, have been far more optimistic on the supply outlook than what's been realized. So I keep that in the back of my mind when I'm looking at the market. There, here and now, crude is backwardated, right?

So refiners are paying a premium. I'm paying a $1 premium to have crude in July versus waiting till August. That's meaningful. And refinery margins, right-- what the refiner gets paid to refine that crude that they're paying premium for today are sitting at 14, 15-month highs.

So there's no indication on the refinery side that there is this capitulation in demand that we're worried about. That's what we're looking at today.

Kim Parlee - Fascinating. Give me 20 seconds on gold.

Hussein Allidina - Oh, well, we like gold. You just switched there. We like gold. We've liked gold for the better part of the last two years, largely on the heels of central bank buying.

Central banks in the emerging market continue to buy gold to diversify away from the fiat, which makes up the bulk of their reserves. We did see weakness in gold over the course of the last couple of weeks because equity performance was strong. So you saw retail moving out of gold into equities. And you saw that in the ETF open interest. It came down.

Ultimately, I think gold needs to be part of your portfolio. Serves a very different purpose than the bulk of the stuff that you already own. And I'd like to be on the side of the central banks that have far deeper pockets and continue to buy gold.

data.title: HealthCare Realty Trust Incorporated: Navigating Portfolio Reshaping; Patience Advised As Market Awaits Clarity
data.description:
Investment Thesis

HR currently trades at $14.29 per share. My analysis of Healthcare Realty Trust (NYSE:HR) reinforces a cautiously optimistic stance after a clearer view of its challenges as well as the nature of its units. The market seems to be gearing for near-term headwinds and some post-merger complexities, creating what I see as a HOLD rating for HR given its current volatility.

  • Core-Asset Stability: HR's portfolio of 650 medical outpatient buildings (MOBs), which are campus-aligned with leading health systems, offers income opportunities due to the essential nature of healthcare services and favorable outpatient trends.
  • In-Depth Financials Reveal Transition: While Q1 2025 showed a GAAP EPS loss of $0.13 per share, Normalized FF) was $0.39 per share, flat YoY. The ongoing $400-$500M asset disposition is temporarily pressuring FFO but is crucial for deleveraging. A detailed look at historical FFO AFFO trends and balance sheet evolution post-HTA merger shows a company in active repositioning.
  • Market and Strategy Alignment: The MOB sector benefits from aging demographics and the shift to outpatient care. HR’s strategy to refine its portfolio and focus on core growth markets, once deleveraging is advanced, should align it well with these favorable long-term trends.
  • Dividend Yield at 8.6%: The $0.31 quarterly dividend (annualized $1.24) provides a significant current yield. My analysis of its coverage (Q1 NFFO payout at 79.5%) and sustainability in the form of asset sales and debt reduction suggests that it is currently supported, though any future dividend growth will depend on successful FFO stabilization and growth post-repositioning.
  • Current Value of HR: HR currently trades at a low Normalized FFO of 9.1x (at $14.20 per share). A more in-depth NAV analysis suggests a material discount to underlying asset values. My DCF model, based on projected stabilized FFO/AFFO, supports a fair value range of $17-$19, indicating some upside but not significant enough for me, so I rate HR a HOLD for now.

Company Overview

HR is a prominent self-managed REIT (Real Estate Investment Trust) specializing in the ownership, management, and development of medical outpatient buildings (MOBs). Its significant scale, with a portfolio of around 650 properties totaling over 38 million square feet as of 2025, largely stems from its 2022 merger with Healthcare Trust of America (HTA).

HR strategically focuses on MOBs located on or adjacent to the campuses of market-leading hospitals across 15 key US markets. This on-campus or campus-aligned strategy is designed to create symbolic relationships with strong health systems and physician tenants who benefit from proximity and referral networks. Currently, HR is in the phase of active portfolio optimization, divesting non-core assets to refine its holdings post-merger.

This strategic emphasis on high-quality, campus-aligned MOBs in growing markets provides a defensive foundation that I believe the market currently does not value and though it may prove fruitful in the long term, a cautious approach is needed in the near term.

Financials

In my analysis of HR’s financials, I focused on discerning underlying operational health between its ongoing strategic repositioning. The Q1 2025 results showed a net loss attributable to common shareholders of approximately $44 million, or $0.13 per share. This GAAP loss was influenced by depreciation and costs related to assets held for sale as part of the disposition program and is a reason for the market's pessimistic view of HR.

More indicative of a REIT would be Normalized Funds From Operations (NFFO) for Q1 2025 was $0.39 per share, flat compared to Q1 2024. This stability in NFFO despite the asset sale should be noted as the company affirmed its full-year NFFO guidance of $1.56 to $1.60 per share, which could be seen as a sign of some stability but should also be approached with some caution.

Historical Trends and HTA Merger Impact:

Looking back, HR’s financial profile has been shaped by the HTA merger. For example, FY2023 NFFO per share was $1.58 and for FY 2024 it was $1.56. Before the merger, both HR and HTA had their own FFO per share trajectories, the current phase is about optimizing the combined entity.

Total revenues for FY 2023 were $1.26 billion, declining slightly to $1.22 billion in FY 2024, reflecting early disposition effects and market conditions as well. The key trend that I have an eye on is the stabilization and eventual growth of FFO AFFO per share after the bulk of the 2025 dispositions are complete and leveraged is reduced but that would be a future long-term play as I don’t see much growth in the near-term.

Balance Sheets and Deleveraging:

As of March 2025, HR had a total debt of almost $5.2 billion, with a Net Debt to Adjusted EBITDA of 6.4x. HR management has been focused on reducing this, targeting $400-$500 million in asset sales in 2025. About $28 million were sold in Q1, with more in progress, their goal according to them is to bring the leverage down to a high 5x to low 6x range, reducing this overall debt is critical in the current interest rate climate, and it remains to be seen if HR is capable of it.

Dividend Sustainability:

The $0.31 quarterly dividend ($1.24 annualized) is a major component for HR to have any bullish case. The Q1 2025 NFFO payout ratio was 79.5%. For FY 2024, with NFFO per share of $1.56 and dividends of $1.24, the payout ratio was similar at 79.5%. This level, while not excessively low, does not leave a big margin for error if FFO were to significantly decline due to aggressive dispositions without offsetting interest savings or growth.

However, if HR achieves its NFFO guidance midpoint of $1.58, then the payout ratio against the $1.24 dividend would remain consistent at 78.5%. Looking into their earnings call for Q1 2025 does reveal any direct statement on dividends, and the emphasis was mostly on dispositions and strengthening the balance sheet to support long-term objectives.

HR's financials show a picture of a company still under transition. While asset sales create short-term FFO headwinds and GAAP losses, the underlying stability in Normalized FFO, coupled with a deleveraging plan, although showing promise for the future, in the short-term, will continue to report similar results and the dividend payout is enough for me, and thus I say HOLD for now for those already in and wait for a more suitable entry point for those looking to buy.

Market Insights and their Strategic Bets

HR’s strategy is firmly rooted in the MOB sector, which benefits from secular tailwinds: an aging U.S. population and the ongoing shift in healthcare delivery to outpatient settings. HR has focused on concentrating on properties in or adjacent to hospital campuses.

HR's Strategic bets Amidst Market Conditions:

  1. Aggressive Deleveraging via Dispositions: The primary current strategy is selling assets in 2025 and reducing its Net Debt. This bet prioritizes balance sheet strength over near-term FFO growth, which will prove positive in the long term.
  2. Core Portfolio Focus: Post-HTA merger, refining the portfolio towards higher-quality, strategically aligned MOBs is key, as this enhances long-term NOI growth potential.
  3. Dividend maintenance: The 8.6% yield is central. The success of the deleveraging plan is critical for its dividend's long-term sustainability and potential future growth.

Looking at HR, they clearly have a plan and are working on it, but in my view, it provides little value to its investors in the near term in terms of dividends and not enough for me to invite new investors and should HOLD on for now.

Unpacking the Risks

Some of the key risks to HR will be discussed here, as I feel they can have an impact on it.

  1. Interest Rate Sensitivity: Higher rates increase borrowing costs and can make HR’s dividend yield relatively less attractive, pressuring its unit price.
  2. Asset Disposition Execution and FFO Dilution: The $400-500 million asset sale plan in 2025, while strategically sound for deleveraging, will likely dilute FFO per share in the near term. Achieving target sales prices is also key.

These risks, particularly interest rate headwinds and the optics of FFO dilution from sales, are central to the current market caution. My thesis is that the eventual deleveraging does not offer much in the short term, and investors need a skeptical approach and buy in at a more favorable entry point.

Valuation

HR currently trades at $14.29 per share with a dividend yield of 8.6%. The company's Normalized FFO guidance is $1.56-$1.60 per share (midpoint $1.58). This puts HR at a Price Normalized FFO (P/FFO) multiple of 9.1x($14.29/$1.58). This is substantially below the historical averages for MOB REITs (12x-18x) and even below its own past ranges, signaling market pessimism.

Net Asset Value (NAV) Analysis:

A common REIT valuation method is comparing the stock price to the estimated NAV per share. MOB cap rates, according to Q1 2025 industry reports, have generally been in the 6 to 7% range for quality, campus-aligned assets. If we take HR's annualized Q1 2025 same-store cash NOI of $192 million and apply a conservative blended cap rate of, say, 6.5% to its total portfolio NOI (after dispositions), then subtract the net debt, the resulting NAV per share estimate lands in the high teens, but the emphasis point here is after dispositions, so this is a long-term play for me. NAV does support a higher valuation, but only after the dispositions are completed, so this makes HR a HOLD in the near term for me.

Discounted Cash Flow (AFFO/FCFE based)/(Multi-stage DDM):

For a REIT, rather than a traditional corporate DCF, I often look at a multi-stage DDM or a DCF of Adjusted Funds From Operation (AFFO) per share, which is FFO less recurring capital expenditures.

  • Current annualized Dividend : $1.24
  • AFFO per share: Typically slightly less than NFFO, if NFFO is $1.58, AFFO is then around $1.40-$1.45 after recurring capex.
  • Short-term AFFO Growth (Post-Disposition Era) Modest 1.2% growth as the portfolio stabilizes and benefits from contractual rent bumps and improving occupancy.
  • Long-term Perpetual Growth: 1-1.5% assumed
  • Cost of Equity: 8.6%
  • AFFO-Based DCF: Projecting AFFO per share with modest growth (1-2%) after 2025's repositioning, then discounting these cash flows and a terminal value at an 8.6% cost of equity. Both of these cash flow-centric approaches, when I model them, support a fair value higher in the long term after the repositioning is complete.

So summing up, I feel all these metrics indicate long-term value for HR and the investors already bought in should hold for now and wait out these current market sentiments and good times seem on the horizon after the repositioning, and should HOLD right now, as for the potential investors looking for an in, this might not be the right time and should wait out when better entry point opens.

Conclusion

My analysis of HR solidifies my HOLD rating with a strong conviction for investors who understand the current transitional phase.

The market appears to be correctly focused on the short-term FFO dilution from its necessary $400-$500 million asset disposition program and the headwinds that come with it and this will and is affecting its current performance in the markets and showing itself in its financial.

My rating of HOLD comes after a thorough analysis which suggests to me that HR is on course for long-term positivity, but in the current and near-term they will continue facing headwinds from the execution of the disposition plan and I advise caution for now.

data.title: Invest Where Private Market Growth Begins
data.description:
Private markets are growing, and the firms managing that capital are at the center of this growth. We make the case for why these firms present an opportunity investors shouldn’t overlook.

What was once the domain of institutions has been opening up to everyday investors, as private equity, credit, real estate, and infrastructure become more accessible. Alternative asset managers, the firms that source, analyze, and allocate to these markets, are expanding beyond pensions and endowments and pushing into wealth management and even retirement accounts. As this shift accelerates, new opportunities are emerging for investors to tap into this rapidly growing space.

Forces Powering the Rise of Alternative Asset Managers

Private assets under management have ballooned at a rapid pace as institutional investors have sought uncorrelated exposure and attractive yield in their long-term portfolio mix. These alternative asset managers advise larger pools of capital, often captive assets with long-term lockups. Their push into new channels such as wealth management and retirement are helping to fuel this growth and also open doors to potential future growth.

Global Alternative Assets Under Management as of 6/30/2024

A Spotlight on Private Credit

Private credit has been a recent standout within private markets. As traditional lenders pull back, alternative managers have stepped in to fill the void, offering customized lending solutions to businesses of all sizes. This trend has shown no signs of slowing.

Global Private Credit AUM ($ trillions) / Projections as of January 2025

Why Companies Are Staying Private Longer

Meanwhile, more companies are choosing to stay private longer. The number of publicly listed companies in the U.S. has dropped since the late 1990s, even as market capitalizations have risen. A concentrated group of mega-cap tech names now dominates public markets, leaving many high-growth companies in private hands.

This shift has elevated the role of alternative asset managers as gatekeepers to some of the most dynamic segments of the economy.

Median Age of VC-Backed Companies (Years)

IPOs Rare Among Recent VC Exits / Share of Total Value in 2024

The Advantage of Captive Capital

One of the more compelling aspects of alternative asset managers is their access to long-term capital. Unlike traditional managers who rely on daily liquidity structures like mutual funds or ETFs, these firms typically operate investment vehicles with multi-year lockups or limited liquidity.

These include:

  • Private funds (LPs, SPVs, etc.)
  • Separately managed accounts
  • Semi-liquid vehicles (interval funds, tender offer funds, BDCs, and REITs)

These structures allow managers to take a long-term view, supporting more stable fee revenues and enabling them to navigate economic cycles.

A Full Spectrum of Private Market Opportunity

Investing in publicly traded alternative asset managers offers exposure across the entire private markets spectrum.

Private Markets Spectrum

Private Equity / Venture Capital Private Credit Private Infrastructure Private Real Estate
Investment in private companies or buyouts of public companies, with goal of increasing value before exiting through a sale or IPO; or exposure to early-stage companies with the goal of outsized returns if a company is successful. Non-bank lending to companies, often in the form of direct loans. Offers investors attractive yields and borrowers customized financing unavailable through traditional lenders. Long-term investments in physical assets like transportation, energy, utilities, and digital infrastructure. These assets often provide stable, inflation-linked cash flows. Direct investment in commercial or residential property, often through private funds. Focuses on income from rents and capital appreciation from asset management and development.

How to Invest in Private Market Managers

The VanEck Alternative Asset Manager ETF (GPZ) offers investors targeted exposure to major companies across the private markets spectrum. Holdings include leading firms like Blackstone, Apollo, and KKR, among other major players in private equity, credit, real estate, infrastructure, and venture capital. GPZ is designed to track the performance of the MarketVector Alternative Asset Managers Index, an ultra pure-play index focused on firms whose core business is managing and growing private capital.

Private markets are transforming global capital flows, and alternative asset managers are at the center of that evolution. With GPZ, investors can now gain exposure to this momentum through a liquid and transparent ETF.
